---
title: "W07-Reflection Essay-Introduction to Positron"
author: "Ian Hamada"
date: "June 28, 2026"
format: 
  html: 
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    number-sections: true
    code-link: true
    embed-resources: true
editor: visual
execute: 
  freeze: auto
---

# Reflection on W07: Introduction to Positron

This report reflects on what I learned from the Introduction to Positron module. I organized the report by prompt so it is clear which response addresses each assignment requirement.

::: callout-note
This report uses Quarto features such as headings, lists, blockquotes, a table, panel tabset, cross-references, a footnote, callout blocks, a table of contents, section numbering, theme options, and self-contained HTML output.
:::

# Project Setup

> **Prompt:** Start a new project and Quarto file (.qmd) inside Positron. Create a folder for this workshop called `Intro-Positron`, open it as the project folder, and create a Quarto file inside the folder.

I created a folder called `Intro-Positron` for this assignment and opened that folder as a project in Positron. When the folder opened as a project, Positron created a `.posit` folder automatically. This helped me understand that Positron keeps project-level settings inside the project folder.

I also created this Quarto file inside the same project folder. Keeping the `.qmd`, rendered `.html`, and screenshot in one project folder makes the assignment easier to organize and submit.

# Essay Prompt Responses

## Prompt 1: Download and Install Positron

> **Prompt:** Download and Install Positron. As you watch the videos in Step 1 and Step 3, follow the activities in the video and be familiar with Positron.

After downloading and installing Positron, I explored the interface and compared it with RStudio. The layout felt familiar because Positron still has an editor, console, and areas for files, variables, plots, and outputs. However, Positron also feels different because it uses a more VS Code-style interface with an activity bar and sidebars.

The Step 1 and Step 3 videos helped me understand how to open a project, run code, inspect objects, and navigate the interface. I learned that the Activity Bar is important because it controls what appears in the side panel. For example, I can use it to access files, search, source control, and other tools.

The biggest adjustment for me was getting used to the Command Palette. In RStudio, I usually look for a button or menu. In Positron, the Command Palette seems more central because I can search for commands instead of trying to remember where everything is located.

::: callout-tip
The main thing I learned from installing and exploring Positron is that it is familiar enough for RStudio users, but it also introduces a more flexible and modern workflow.
:::

## Prompt 2: What I Like About Positron Compared with RStudio

> **Prompt:** Based on what you learned from Step 1 and Step 3, what do you like about Positron compared with RStudio?

What I like most about Positron compared with RStudio is that it feels more flexible. RStudio is easier for me right now because I am used to it, but Positron feels more modern and better suited for working with multiple tools. Since Positron supports both R and Python, I can see how it would be useful for data science projects that use more than one language.

I also like the Activity Bar and sidebars. At first, they looked different from RStudio, but after using them, I understood that they help organize the workspace. Instead of having every pane fixed in one layout, Positron lets me move through different tools depending on what I am doing.

Another feature I liked is the Command Palette. It makes it easier to search for actions such as opening settings, rendering a document, or finding a command. This could make me faster once I get used to it.

| Feature | RStudio | Positron |
|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Main workflow | Familiar R-focused IDE | Modern R and Python data science IDE |
| Interface | Fixed panes and menus | Activity Bar, sidebars, editor, and panels |
| Commands | Mostly buttons and menus | Command Palette is more central |
| Languages | Strongest for R | Designed for R and Python |
| AI support | Less central | AI tools can be integrated into the workflow |

: Comparison of RStudio and Positron features. {#tbl-comparison}

As shown in @tbl-comparison, Positron keeps some familiar IDE features while adding a more flexible workflow.

## Prompt 3: AI Inside Positron

> **Prompt:** In Step 4, the video demonstrates how you can use AI. Describe the various ways you can use AI inside Positron. Some are free while others are not.

AI can be used inside Positron in several ways. One major tool is Positron Assistant, which can help with code generation, debugging, explanations, and data analysis tasks. This is useful because the help can happen directly inside the IDE instead of switching back and forth between Positron and a browser.

Another AI-related feature discussed in the module is Databot. Databot is useful for exploratory data analysis because it can help inspect data, identify patterns, and suggest code-based analysis steps. I like that this still keeps the user in control because the AI gives suggestions, but the user still has to review and run the code.

GitHub Copilot can also be connected to Positron. Copilot can suggest code while typing, help complete functions, and generate examples. This can be useful for remembering syntax, especially when writing Quarto, `ggplot2`, or data wrangling code.

Some AI tools are free, while others are paid. GitHub Copilot is free for students who are approved through GitHub Education, but otherwise it may require a paid subscription. Other AI tools may also require an account or paid access depending on the provider.

::: panel-tabset
## Helpful AI Uses

AI inside Positron can help with:

- writing starter code
- debugging errors
- explaining functions
- creating example data
- building charts
- suggesting exploratory data analysis steps

## Possible AI Problems

AI can also be distracting if I rely on it too much. It may suggest code that looks correct but does not actually match the assignment. It can also make me move too quickly without understanding the code.
:::

## Prompt 4: AI Tools Installed or Set Up

> **Prompt:** Which AI tools have you installed or set up? Which AI tools did you find beneficial for you?

The AI tool I focused on setting up was GitHub Copilot because the assignment strongly recommended it and because it is free for students who are approved through GitHub Education. My GitHub Education application was approved, but GitHub stated that benefits, including Copilot access, may take up to 72 hours to become available. Because of that, I could not fully use Copilot immediately.

I still found GitHub Copilot to be the most beneficial AI tool based on the module videos and the setup process. Copilot can help with coding syntax, completing functions, debugging, and generating examples. For example, it could help me remember how to start a `ggplot2` chart, write a data wrangling step, or format a Quarto document.

I also learned about Positron Assistant and Databot. Positron Assistant can help with code generation and explanations, while Databot can support exploratory data analysis. However, GitHub Copilot seems like the tool I would use most often because it works directly while coding.

Overall, I think Copilot would be useful as a support tool, but I would not want to rely on it too much. I still need to understand, test, and edit the code myself.

## Prompt 5: GitHub Copilot Education Account and Reflection

> **Prompt:** I strongly recommend using GitHub Copilot, which is free if you apply for an education account. Apply for it and take a screenshot showing you were accepted into the education program. Play with it and do you find it helpful or distracting? Please elaborate.

I applied for GitHub Education so that I could access GitHub Copilot as a student. My academic status was approved, and GitHub stated that my benefits, including Partner offers and Copilot access, will become available after 72 hours.

![GitHub Education or GitHub Copilot approval screenshot](images/Approval Screenshot.png){#fig-copilot}

As shown in @fig-copilot, my GitHub Education application was approved. Since the benefits are still waiting to become available, I may not have full Copilot access immediately, but the approval confirms that I completed the required application step.

Based on what I learned from the video, I think GitHub Copilot would be helpful because it can suggest code, complete functions, help with syntax, and support debugging directly inside the coding environment. This would be useful when writing Quarto, creating visualizations, or working through data analysis code.

At the same time, I think Copilot could become distracting if I accepted suggestions too quickly without understanding them. For me, Copilot is best used as a support tool, not as a replacement for learning. I would still need to read the code, test it, and make sure it matches the assignment requirements.[^1]

[^1]: AI-generated code should always be checked because it can be incomplete, incorrect, or unrelated to the assignment requirements.

::: {.callout-tip}
My GitHub Education application was approved. Copilot benefits may take up to 72 hours to become available, so I included the approval screenshot as proof of completion.
:::

## Prompt 6: Publish to GitHub Pages

> **Prompt:** Publish this report to GitHub Pages and provide a URL to the GitHub Pages for the report. Note that although both GitHub Pages and GitHub repo are online, they are different. GitHub Pages is a website publishing service that hosts the rendered HTML from a QMD file.

After rendering this Quarto file to HTML, I will publish the report using GitHub Pages. The GitHub repository stores the project files, including the `.qmd`, `.html`, and image file. GitHub Pages is different because it displays the rendered HTML report as a public website.

My GitHub Pages URL is:

> **https://imhamada1.github.io/W07-Introduction-to-Positron/**

::: callout-important
The GitHub repository is where the files are stored. GitHub Pages is the published website version of the rendered HTML report.
:::

# Final Reflection

Overall, this module helped me understand Positron as a modern IDE for data science. I still feel more comfortable with RStudio because I have used it more, but Positron seems useful because it supports R, Python, Quarto, Git, and AI tools in one environment.

The biggest thing I learned is that Positron is not just a copy of RStudio. It has a different workflow, especially with the Activity Bar, sidebars, Command Palette, and AI integrations. I think Positron could be helpful for future projects, especially if I need to combine coding, writing, data analysis, version control, and AI support in one place.
